game_name: Street Fighter II Turbo: Hyper Fighting

design_philosophy.core_loop: Select character → fight sequential opponents with increasing difficulty → master matchups through frame-data precision and special move execution
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Player execution precision vs. opponent AI/player adaptation; risk/reward of committing to special moves and combos with tighter input windows
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Become a master martial artist through practice and muscle memory, executing flashy special moves to defeat a global roster of iconic fighters
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Emphasizes execution-based skill through deliberately compressed input timing and faster game speed; rewards deep character knowledge and frame-perfect inputs over button mashing; each character receives unique move sets reflecting their fighting philosophy

difficulty.lives_system: Single life per character in arcade ladder; continue system allows progression after defeat
difficulty.continues_system: Continue available after losing; player can retry fallen opponents
difficulty.checkpointing: Sequential fighting ladder with no mid-ladder saves; progression restarts on game over
difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: SNES port offers adjustable game speed via four default settings plus cheat codes for up to six faster speeds; cheat codes allow players to disable special moves for practice; selectable difficulty implicitly available through character selection and opponent selection

progression.unlock_mechanism: Character roster determined at character select screen; no progression-based unlocks documented
replayability_hooks: 16 playable characters with entirely distinct move sets and special moves
Versus mode for competitive multiplayer rematches
Adjustable speed settings in SNES port encourage mastering execution at different tempos
Deep matchup knowledge incentivizes replaying against specific opponent characters

world_building_techniques: Character selection screen displays country of origin for each fighter
Fighter names and visual designs reference international martial arts traditions

boss_design.philosophy: Tournament-style gauntlet with escalating opponent difficulty; final opponents (Grand Masters: Ryu, Ken, Chun-Li, Zangief) represent the skill ceiling
notable_bosses: Grand Masters (Ryu, Ken, Chun-Li, Zangief) - final opponents with retained Champion Edition rules
M. Bison - final boss with status as series antagonist

faction_indicators: Country of origin for each fighter (Japanese, Chinese, Indian, Brazilian, American, Soviet, Thai, Spanish, Italian, UK, African, Native American origins)
Fighting style archetypes (grappler, zoner, rushdown, teleporter)
danger_signals: M. Bison as final antagonist suggests power struggle and dominance hierarchy
Grand Masters serve as skill gatekeepers reflecting martial arts tournament structure
environmental_storytelling: Fighter visual designs embed cultural identity and fighting tradition
Stage backgrounds vary by character origin, grounding each fighter in geography
